notes.summary: Prime factorisation is the process of breaking down a number into its prime factors, which are the prime numbers that multiply together to give the original number. This can be done by finding the product of two factors and then finding their prime factorisations separately. The prime factorisation of a number has one 2, two 3s, and one 11, for example.
notes.importance: Prime factorisation is fundamental in mathematics as it helps us understand the properties of numbers and is used extensively in algebra, geometry, and number theory.
quick_facts: Numbers can be broken down into their prime factors
Each prime factor appears a certain number of times in the factorisation
The product of the prime factors gives the original number
Prime factorisation is used to check if two numbers are co-prime

notes.summary: Two numbers are said to be co-prime if they have no common prime factors. This can be checked by finding the prime factorisations of both numbers and looking for any shared factors. If there are no common prime factors, then the two numbers are co-prime.
notes.importance: Co-primes are important in mathematics as they help us understand the properties of numbers and are used extensively in number theory and cryptography.
quick_facts: Two numbers are co-prime if they have no common prime factors
Prime factorisation is used to check if two numbers are co-prime
Co-primes are used extensively in number theory and cryptography
Example: 40 and 231 are co-prime as they have no common prime factors

notes.summary: A number is divisible by 4 if the last two digits form a number that is itself divisible by 4. This can be checked by finding the prime factorisation of the number and looking for any factors of 2^2.
notes.importance: Divisibility by 4 is important in mathematics as it helps us understand the properties of numbers and is used extensively in arithmetic and algebra.
quick_facts: A number is divisible by 4 if its last two digits form a number that is itself divisible by 4
Prime factorisation can be used to check divisibility by 4
Example: 36 is divisible by 4 as it has a factor of 2^2
Important property: if the original number is divisible by 4, then the number formed by the last two digits is also divisible by 4

notes.summary: Twin primes are pairs of prime numbers that have a difference of 2. Examples include (3,5), (17,19), and (29,31). Twin primes are interesting as they can help us understand the distribution of prime numbers.
notes.importance: Twin primes are important in mathematics as they help us understand the properties of prime numbers and their distribution.
quick_facts: Twin primes are pairs of prime numbers with a difference of 2
Twin primes can help us understand the distribution of prime numbers
Interesting property: twin primes have a unique relationship to each other

notes.summary: Some prime numbers have special properties, such as being co-prime with certain other numbers or having specific patterns in their digits. For example, 2 is the only even prime number and all other primes are odd.
notes.importance: Prime numbers with special properties are important in mathematics as they help us understand the unique characteristics of these numbers.
quick_facts: Some prime numbers have special properties
Example: 2 is the only even prime number
All other primes are odd
Important property: if a number has a unit digit of 4, it cannot be prime

notes.summary: In multiplication, the order in which we perform operations does not matter. This is known as commutativity and associativity. For example, (2 × 3) × 5 = 2 × (3 × 5).
notes.importance: Commutativity and associativity are important in mathematics as they help us understand the properties of numbers and operations.
quick_facts: In multiplication, order does not matter
This property is known as commutativity and associativity
Important in arithmetic and algebra

notes.concept_name: Prime Factorisation of Products of Numbers
notes.summary: When we find the prime factorisation of a product of two numbers, we can simply multiply their individual prime factorisations. For example, 72 = (2^3 × 3) × (2 × 3).
notes.importance: Prime factorisation of products is important in mathematics as it helps us understand the properties of numbers and operations.
quick_facts: When finding prime factorisation of a product, multiply individual prime factorisations
This property is useful in arithmetic and algebra
Important for understanding number properties
